name: Helvetica
classification_notes: Originated as Neue Haas Grotesk at Haas Type Foundry; Linotype licensed and renamed it Helvetica in 1960. One of the most widely used typefaces in the world. High x-height, tight spacing, horizontal stroke terminals.
summary: The defining neo-grotesque sans-serif of the 20th century, originally designed for Haas and distributed globally by Linotype.
description: Helvetica began life as Neue Haas Grotesk in 1957, designed by Max Miedinger under Eduard Hoffmann at the Haas Type Foundry in Switzerland. Linotype licensed it and renamed it Helvetica in 1960, distributing it across typesetting machines worldwide. Its neutral, clean character made it the default corporate typeface of the late 20th century, used by brands, governments and transit systems across the globe.
foundry_blurb: Helvetica is one of the most famous and widely used typefaces in the world, celebrated for its clean, modern forms and exceptional versatility across media and scales.

name: Univers
classification_notes: Released by Deberny & Peignot in 1957; Linotype later acquired and reworked the family as Linotype Univers (1997) with 63 fonts and a three-digit numbering system. Based on 19th-century grotesque models.
summary: Adrian Frutiger's systematically organised sans-serif family, revolutionary for its comprehensive range of weights and widths at launch.
description: Univers was designed by Adrian Frutiger and released by Deberny & Peignot in 1957, the same year as Helvetica. Unlike its rival, Univers was conceived from the outset as a large, internally consistent system — Frutiger assigned each variant a two-digit number to map weight and width. Linotype reworked the entire family in 1997, expanding it to 63 fonts with a revised numbering scheme.
foundry_blurb: Univers is a landmark of 20th-century type design, offering an unprecedented systematic range of weights and widths that set the template for large sans-serif superfamilies.

name: Frutiger
classification_notes: Originally designed for signage at Charles de Gaulle Airport, Paris. Wide open apertures, high x-height, oblique rather than true italic. Released to the public by Stempel and Linotype.
summary: A humanist sans-serif designed for maximum legibility in signage, named after its creator Adrian Frutiger.
description: Frutiger was commissioned for the directional signage system at Charles de Gaulle Airport and released publicly by Stempel and Linotype in 1976. Its wide, open apertures and high x-height make it exceptionally legible at distance and in adverse conditions. It became the standard for transport signage, pharmaceutical labelling and corporate communications worldwide.
foundry_blurb: Frutiger is Adrian Frutiger's most humanist sans-serif — designed for clarity at distance, with open counters and a warmth that distinguishes it from the colder Univers.

name: Avenir
classification_notes: Geometric sans-serif with organic corrections — optically adjusted letterforms rather than strict geometry. Frutiger called it his masterpiece. Draws from Futura and Erbar but with humanist refinements.
summary: Adrian Frutiger's geometric sans-serif with organic refinements, released by Linotype in 1988 and later bundled by Apple as a system font.
description: Avenir was designed by Adrian Frutiger and released by Linotype in 1988, drawing on the 1920s geometric tradition while correcting the mechanical stiffness of pure geometric typefaces. Frutiger described it as a more organic interpretation and considered it his masterpiece. Avenir Next, developed with Akira Kobayashi, expanded the family to 24 fonts and added condensed widths.
foundry_blurb: Avenir — French for 'future' — is Frutiger's personal take on the geometric sans-serif, refined over decades and widely used in corporate and civic identity.

name: Palatino
classification_notes: Italian Renaissance humanist serif. Named after Renaissance calligrapher Giambattista Palatino. Open counters, balanced proportions, moderate stroke contrast and flared serifs. Originally designed for display, widely adopted for body text.
summary: Hermann Zapf's Renaissance-inspired serif, one of the ten most-used typefaces in the world and a standard on PostScript systems.
description: Palatino was designed by Hermann Zapf and released by the Stempel foundry in 1949, drawing on Italian Renaissance letterforms and Zapf's own calligraphic training. Its open counters and flared serifs suit both display and body text use. Linotype distributed it widely and it became one of the first typefaces licensed for PostScript, making it a default on Apple and Adobe systems.
foundry_blurb: Palatino is Hermann Zapf's most celebrated book face — a humanist serif rooted in Italian Renaissance calligraphy, with a warmth and authority that has kept it in constant use for over 70 years.

name: Optima
classification_notes: Classified as humanist sans-serif but features subtle stroke modulation at terminals suggesting a glyphic quality — neither fully serif nor fully sans. Inspired by classical Roman capitals and Renaissance tombstones. Proportions based on the Golden Section.
summary: Hermann Zapf's elegant hybrid — a sans-serif with subtle stroke modulation that evokes Roman classical inscriptions.
description: Optima was released by Stempel and Linotype in 1958, inspired by classical Roman stonecutting that Zapf had observed in Florence. Its flared terminals hover between sans-serif and serifed letter, giving it a classical gravity rare in the sans genre. Optima nova (2002) added true italics and seven weights. It appears on the Vietnam Veterans Memorial and the National September 11 Memorial.
foundry_blurb: Optima is a typeface of rare distinction — Zapf's attempt to capture the modulated stroke of Roman inscriptions within a clean, modern sans-serif structure.

name: Sabon
classification_notes: Old-style serif based on Claude Garamond's 16th-century designs, italics derived from Robert Granjon. Jointly released by Linotype, Monotype and Stempel. Designed with uniform widths across roman, italic and bold to satisfy Linotype's duplex casting requirements.
summary: Jan Tschichold's definitive Garamond revival, jointly developed with Linotype, Monotype and Stempel for precise body-text use.
description: Sabon was commissioned in the 1960s to provide a Garamond revival that could work identically across different compositing systems. Tschichold based the roman on Claude Garamond and the italic on Robert Granjon, keeping widths uniform across styles to satisfy the technical constraints of Linotype's machines. It remains among the most trusted text faces for literary and liturgical publishing.
foundry_blurb: Sabon is one of the great book typefaces — a disciplined Garamond revival designed by Jan Tschichold that brings Renaissance elegance to modern production requirements.

name: Trade Gothic
classification_notes: American grotesque based on Akzidenz-Grotesk. More irregular and airy than Helvetica or Univers — designed incrementally rather than as a systematic family. Expanded to 14 style-weight combinations by 1960.
summary: An American grotesque with a looser, more irregular character than its Swiss contemporaries, designed at Mergenthaler Linotype from 1948.
description: Trade Gothic was developed by Jackson Burke at Mergenthaler Linotype starting in 1948, growing incrementally rather than as a planned system. Its slightly uneven proportions give it more personality than Helvetica — designers often describe it as airy and characterful. Trade Gothic Next (2009), redesigned by Akira Kobayashi, cleaned up inconsistencies while preserving the original's charm.
foundry_blurb: Trade Gothic is Linotype's quintessential American grotesque — purposeful, unpretentious and full of personality precisely because it was never over-engineered.

name: Syntax
classification_notes: Humanist sans-serif with wide uppercase proportions and non-baseline-parallel terminals. Two-storey a and g link it to older typeface traditions. Digitised by Adobe in 1989; extensively revised by Meier and Linotype from 1995, released 2000.
summary: Hans Eduard Meier's humanist sans-serif, released by Stempel in 1969 and extensively reworked with Linotype for the digital era.
description: Syntax was first released by D. Stempel in 1969 and revised comprehensively by Meier in collaboration with Linotype from 1995 onwards. Its wide uppercase proportions, open construction and two-storey lowercase a and g make it more classically grounded than most sans-serifs of its era. The reworked digital version expanded weights and added Cyrillic support.
foundry_blurb: Syntax bridges the worlds of classical serif proportion and modern sans-serif clarity — a Swiss humanist designed with a scholar's rigour and a craftsman's eye.

name: Trump Mediaeval
classification_notes: Old-style serif initially produced by C. E. Weber and Linotype. 'Mediaeval' refers to the German term for dark, roman old-style letterforms — not blackletter. Used for George R. R. Martin's A Song of Ice and Fire novels.
summary: Georg Trump's characterful old-style serif, jointly released by C. E. Weber and Linotype in 1954 and used in George R. R. Martin's fantasy novels.
description: Trump Mediaeval takes its name from the German typographic term for darkly coloured roman typefaces inspired by Venetian old-style models. Designed by Georg Trump and released in 1954, it has a robust personality and was used in the 1975 Gotteslob Catholic hymnal. Digital versions are available from Linotype and Adobe.
foundry_blurb: Trump Mediaeval is a sturdy, characterful old-style — darkly coloured and firmly drawn, with roots in the Venetian tradition and a distinctly German approach to proportion.

name: Aldus
classification_notes: Book-weight companion to Palatino — more condensed and lighter in colour. Named after Aldus Manutius though Zapf preferred to call it Palatino Book. Non-kerning roman and italic f to avoid ligature requirements. Aldus Nova released 2005.
summary: Zapf's refined, lighter companion to Palatino, designed specifically for high-quality book printing.
description: Aldus was designed by Hermann Zapf in 1954 as a leaner, more refined companion to Palatino, intended for book-length text rather than display use. Its more condensed proportions and lighter colour suit smaller sizes and longer texts. Aldus Nova, released in 2005, brought the design up to OpenType standards alongside Palatino Nova.
foundry_blurb: Aldus is Zapf's distillation of Palatino's principles into a dedicated book face — more condensed, more refined and perfectly weighted for body text.

name: Linotype Didot
classification_notes: Digital revival of the Didot tradition designed by Frutiger specifically for display use. Extreme contrast between thick and thin strokes. Not intended for body text at small sizes. Bundled with macOS.
summary: Adrian Frutiger's digital revival of the Didot tradition, refined for display use and noted for extreme stroke contrast.
description: Linotype Didot is a 1991 revival by Adrian Frutiger, designed expressly for display and headline use where the dramatic contrast between thick and thin strokes can be appreciated at scale. Frutiger's version is particularly elegant — clean and controlled rather than overwrought. A standard system font on macOS, it is widely used in fashion publishing and luxury branding.
foundry_blurb: Linotype Didot brings the neoclassical French tradition into the digital age — a display face of austere elegance with extreme stroke contrast and fine hairline details.
